summary: Gruber contrasts the 2003 Apple press release announcing Al Gore's appointment to Apple's board with the 2024 announcement of new board member Wanda Austin. He argues that the 2003 statements from Steve Jobs and Gore himself were specific, personal, and captured the moment — Jobs noting Gore was a Mac enthusiast who used Final Cut Pro, Gore citing Mac OS X and open source. The 2024 statements from Tim Cook and Arthur Levinson are generic corporate platitudes that could apply to any tech company. Gruber also defends Gore's actual record on the internet, noting that internet pioneers Vint Cerf and Robert Kahn credited Gore as the first political leader to recognize and promote the internet's importance.
key_points: Steve Jobs's 2003 announcement about Gore was personal and specific — mentioning his Mac enthusiasm and Final Cut Pro use — while Tim Cook's 2024 announcement about Wanda Austin is interchangeable corporate boilerplate.
Gore's own 2003 statement referenced Mac OS X and open source, capturing the actual moment in Apple's history, whereas Austin's statement could apply to joining any tech company's board.

The 'Al Gore invented the internet' claim was political mockery, not reality — Gore said he 'took the initiative' in supporting the internet's development, which internet pioneers Cerf and Kahn confirmed was accurate.
The contrast illustrates how Apple's public communications have shifted from specific and characterful under Jobs to generic and corporate under Cook.
Gore served on Apple's board for over 20 years and is stepping down due to a mandatory retirement age policy — yet he's still younger than both Biden and Trump.
money_quotes: I love that the second sentence from Jobs was about how Gore is a Mac enthusiast who uses Final Cut Pro.
In 2003 there was one person in the world who could be described as a former vice president of the United States and avid Mac user.
Cook's and Levinson's descriptions of Austin could apply to just about any technology company executive in the world.
She could have said the exact same thing about joining the board of any tech company in the world today.
No one in the year 2044 is going to look back on her statement above and think, Yeah, that captures what was then the current moment for Apple.
Austin may well be a perfect candidate to serve on Apple's board, but there's nothing in today's press release that indicates why.

It's wild to think that Gore, whose ill-fated lost-by-a-hair run for president took place a quarter century ago, has reached the mandatory retirement age for Apple's board, yet is several years younger than both Joe Biden and Donald Trump.
key_insight: Apple's corporate communications have devolved from specific, personality-driven statements under Jobs to generic, interchangeable platitudes under Cook, losing the ability to capture what makes a moment or a person distinctive.
